retrieved_docs.title: Galantamine -- Adverse Effects -- Warning and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Gastrointestinal: Galantamine is a well-tolerated dementia medication. However, gastrointestinal adverse events are commonly experienced by patients taking medications with cholinergic properties. The most common gastrointestinal adverse effects include loss of appetite, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and weight loss. [3] [46] [47]

retrieved_docs.title: Galantamine -- Adverse Effects -- Warning and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Due to increased parasympathetic activity, Galantamine may increase gastric acid secretion through its primary action. Patients should be closely monitored for symptoms of active or occult gastrointestinal bleeding, particularly those with additional risk factors for developing ulcers, such as a history of peptic ulcer disease or recent nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs. Clinical trials with galantamine have not demonstrated any increased incidence of peptic ulcer disease or gastrointestinal bleeding compared to placebo.

retrieved_docs.title: Galantamine -- Adverse Effects -- Warning and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: The patient's weight must be monitored during galantamine therapy. For reduced doses, gastrointestinal side effects are usually mild, transient, and reduced in severity. Clinical trials have shown that the median duration of nausea and vomiting is 5 to 6 days after initiating therapy and following each increment in the dosage. Significant cholinergic side effects can develop in patients receiving elevated doses and are usually related to the rapid rate of initial dosage titration. To reduce these unpleasant effects, dosage should be escalated gradually. Additionally, galantamine should be taken with meals. Moreover, the patient and caregiver should be counseled to ensure adequate fluid intake.

retrieved_docs.title: Galantamine -- Adverse Effects -- Warning and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Common adverse reactions observed in patients treated with galantamine in double-blind clinical trials, occurring in at least 5% of cases, included nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, dizziness, headache, and decreased appetite. Other adverse effects reported during clinical trials include depression, anxiety, nightmares, tremors, drowsiness, syncope, lethargy, abdominal discomfort, abdominal pain, dyspepsia, muscle spasms, fatigue, asthenia, malaise, falls, laceration, dehydration, dysgeusia, hypersomnia, paresthesia, blurred vision, retching, hyperhidrosis, rhinorrhea, muscle weakness, palpitations, supraventricular extrasystole, flushing, and hypotension. [48] Pisa syndrome has been reported while switching from donepezil to galantamine. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Galantamine -- Adverse Effects -- Warning and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamine and Mild Cognitive Impairment: Per the data from the 2 separate clinical trials for patients with mild cognitive impairment, galantamine can cause marginal clinical benefit but is still associated with an unexplained excessive death rate. Therefore, galantamine use in patients with a history of mild cognitive impairment is not recommended.

retrieved_docs.title: Galantamine -- Toxicity -- Signs and Symptoms of Overdose
retrieved_docs.content: As in any overdose case, general supportive therapy should be provided. Since the policies for the management of drug overdose are continually advancing, it is advisable to consult a poison control center for the latest recommendations for managing galantamine overdose. The clinical picture of a significant galantamine overdose is similar to an overdose on other cholinergic medications. These effects involve the neuromuscular junction, parasympathetic, and central nervous systems. An overdose of galantamine produces a syndrome of cholinergic crisis. In addition to muscle weakness or fasciculations, the patient may present with some or all the following symptoms and signs: salivation, severe nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramping, sweating, lacrimation, urination, defecation, bradycardia, hypotension, depression of respiration, collapse, and convulsions. Death may occur if worsening muscle weakness involves respiratory muscles.

retrieved_docs.title: Galantamine -- Adverse Effects -- Warning and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Nervous: Headache and dizziness are the most common side effects. Galantamine is believed to induce generalized seizures. The convulsive activity could be a manifestation of Alzheimer disease. Therefore, patients with Alzheimer dementia on galantamine should be monitored closely for seizures.

retrieved_docs.title: Galantamine -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatic impairment: Following ingestion of a single 4 mg dose of galantamine immediate-release tablets in patients with mild hepatic dysfunction (Child-Pugh score of 5 to 6) and healthy volunteers, there was no appreciable difference in the pharmacokinetics of galantamine. In subjects with moderate hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh score of 7 to 9), a 25% reduction in metabolic clearance compared to normal volunteers was observed. Galantamine exposure would be expected to increase with the severity of hepatic impairment. For patients with moderately severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh score of 7 to 9), caution must be exercised during dose titration, and the maximum daily dose should typically not exceed 16 mg daily. Severe liver impairment (Child-Pugh score 10 to 15) is a contraindication for galantamine, immediate or extended-release. Galantamine clearance was decreased by 60% in patients with moderate or severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh score of 7 or higher). Various simulations evaluated the effect of hepatic dysfunction on the peak plasma concentration of galantamine. These simulations confirmed the need for a reduced rate of dose escalation in patients with impaired liver function: 4 mg daily during the first week followed by 4 mg, 8 mg, and 12 mg twice daily dosage compared to the standard dosage titration of 4 mg, 8 mg, 12 mg, 16 mg twice daily.

retrieved_docs.title: Galantamine -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: Ketoconazole and erythromycin are known CYP3A4 inhibitors that reduce the clearance of the galantamine when administered with galantamine. Ketoconazole, paroxetine, amitriptyline, fluoxetine, fluvoxamine, and quinidine all inhibit CYP2D6, and when galantamine is concurrently administered, the oral bioavailability of galantamine increases. When H2-receptor antagonists cimetidine and galantamine are co-administered, galantamine bioavailability increases.

retrieved_docs.title: Galantamine -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: Memantine, an N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor antagonist, does not affect the pharmacokinetics of oral galantamine at 16 mg daily at a steady state when co-administered at 10 mg twice daily.

retrieved_docs.title: Galantamine -- Adverse Effects -- Warning and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Skin: Serious cutaneous adverse reactions such as acute generalized exanthematous pustulosis, erythema multiforme, and Stevens-Johnson syndrome have been reported in patients receiving immediate or extended-release formulations. Patients and caregivers should be advised to discontinue galantamine at the first appearance of a rash unless it is known to have a separate cause. If the patient's clinical presentation suggests a severe dermatological reaction, galantamine therapy should not be resumed, and alternative treatment should be considered.

retrieved_docs.title: Galantamine -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: Anticholinergic medications can interfere with the pharmacological actions of galantamine. Drugs known to induce hepatic cytochrome P450 CYP3A4 or CYP2D6 enzymes, such as carbamazepine phenytoin, phenobarbital, rifampin, or dexamethasone, accelerate the metabolism of galantamine.

retrieved_docs.title: Galantamine -- Administration -- Adult Dosage
retrieved_docs.content: For immediate-release tablets, the dosage of 16 to 32 mg daily has demonstrated effectiveness in a controlled clinical trial for Alzheimer dementia. However, the 32 mg daily dose has been reported to be less tolerable than lower doses. Therefore, the recommended dosage is 16 to 24 mg daily, taken in 2 separate doses with food to reduce gastrointestinal adverse events such as nausea and vomiting. The recommendation is to initiate treatment at the lowest dose and titrate slowly after assessing the patient for clinical benefits and tolerability of the previous dosage. Treatment begins at 4 mg twice daily and should be taken in the morning and evening with food. After a minimum of 4 weeks, galantamine can be increased to 16 mg daily, which is considered a maintenance dose. A further increase to 24 mg daily can be attempted after a minimum of 4 weeks at 16 mg. If possible, the patient should be on 16 mg daily as 24 mg daily has not demonstrated significantly increased efficacy during clinical trials. However, a daily dose of 24 mg may offer significant clinical benefits in some patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Galantamine -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple doses of galantamine at 24 mg daily have no effect on the steady-state pharmacokinetics of digoxin (at a dose of 0.375 mg once daily). Still, patients have been hospitalized for second and complete heart blocks.

retrieved_docs.title: Galantamine -- Indications -- Non-FDA-approved uses of galantamine are listed below:
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamine is effective in the management of acute scopolamine toxicity [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Galantamine -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding considerations: No data is available on galantamine secretion in human milk, its effects on breastfed infants, or the effects of galantamine extended-release or galantamine immediate release on milk production. The clinician must weigh the benefits against the risk of side effects before prescribing galantamine. Pediatric patients: Clinical trials have not been conducted in the pediatric population to establish safety and efficacy. Older patients: According to the data from clinical trials involving patients with Alzheimer disease, galantamine concentrations are 30%-40% higher in older patients than in healthy young subjects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1714
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamin is classified as a parasympathomimetic agent, meaning it mimics the activity of the parasympathetic nervous system by enhancing cholinergic neurotransmission. As a member of the parasympathomimetic class, galantamin exerts its therapeutic effects primarily through reversible inhibition of acetylcholinesterase and allosteric modulation of nicotinic acetylcholine receptors, actions that support cognitive function in conditions such as Alzheimer disease. Recognizing galantamin’s placement within the parasympathomimetic category helps clinicians understand its mechanism of action, potential side‑effect profile, and appropriate use in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1374
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamin and bethanechol are both classified as parasympathomimetics, a group of agents that mimic the activity of the parasympathetic nervous system. By stimulating cholinergic pathways, these drugs enhance parasympathetic tone, which can be therapeutically useful in conditions where increased muscarinic activity is desired. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action despite being used for different clinical indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1389
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamin is classified as a parasympathomimetic agent.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2685
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is identified as a category that includes akamprosát, galantamin and parasympathomimetics in relation to toxicita, indicating that each of these agents is recognized as a qualifier for toxicity considerations. In addition, galantamin is classified as a parasympathomimetic, linking it directly to the broader group of parasympathomimetic substances that are relevant to toxicological assessment. This interconnected framework highlights that akamprosát, galantamin and other parasympathomimetics are all pertinent when evaluating toxicita, and that galantamin’s specific pharmacologic class further defines its role within this toxicity‑related context.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1540
retrieved_docs.content: Domperidone, a dopamine‑antagonist medication commonly used for its anti‑emetic and gastrointestinal pro‑kinetic effects, may have its associated adverse effects or reduced efficacy mitigated when a substantial, heavy breakfast (فطار غزوي) is consumed, suggesting that taking the drug with a large morning meal can help prevent potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1504
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamin and edrofonium are both classified as parasympathomimetic agents, meaning they mimic the activity of the parasympathetic nervous system. As members of this pharmacologic class, they enhance cholinergic transmission and can be used to support functions mediated by acetylcholine, such as cognition, memory, and certain autonomic processes. Recognizing both galantamin and edrofonium as parasympathomimetics helps clinicians understand their mechanism of action and potential therapeutic applications in conditions where augmentation of parasympathetic activity is desired.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1581
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamin is classified as a parasympatomimetic agent, placing it within the group of drugs that imitate the actions of the parasympathetic nervous system. Parasympatomimetika themselves are a subset of autonomní látky (autonomic substances), which encompass all pharmacologic agents that affect autonomic nervous system function. By acting as a parasympatomimetic, galantamin contributes to the modulation of cholinergic pathways and therefore shares the broader physiological effects characteristic of autonomic substances. Understanding galantamin’s position within the parasympatomimetic class and its relationship to autonomní látky aids clinicians in anticipating its pharmacodynamic profile and potential therapeutic or adverse effects related to autonomic regulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 58
retrieved_docs.content: before prescribing. These relationships delineate how individual drugs are organized into functional subclasses, describe their mechanisms (antispasmodic, anticholinergic, pro‑kinetic, serotonin antagonism), specify formulation components, and highlight key contraindications that guide safe and effective patient care in functional gastrointestinal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1824
retrieved_docs.content: H2‑receptor antagonists constitute a class of drugs that act as histamine H2 antagonists, sharing the broader pharmacologic relationship of inhibiting gastric acid secretion via blockade of the H2 histamine receptor. Specific agents that belong to this class include ranitidine, nizatidine, and roxatidine, each representing an individual member of the H2‑receptor antagonist group. Understanding that these medications are unified by their mechanism of H2‑receptor blockade helps clinicians select appropriate therapy for conditions such as peptic ulcer disease and gastro‑oesophageal reflux, while recognizing the shared therapeutic effects and potential class‑related adverse profiles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2558
retrieved_docs.content: Drugs for functional gastrointestinal disorders are classified as propulsives, a group of medications that enhance gastrointestinal motility and are used to manage functional GI conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2288
retrieved_docs.content: Antacids are agents that neutralize gastric acid and are classified within a broader pharmacological parent category that describes drug actions at the molecular level. In clinical practice, antacids are considered contraindicated when used together with famotidin, an H₂‑receptor antagonist, because concurrent administration can interfere with famotidin’s ability to reduce acid secretion and may lead to reduced therapeutic effectiveness or adverse interactions. Understanding that antacids belong to a parent group of molecular‑mechanism‑based drug effects, while also recognizing their contraindication with famotidin, helps clinicians select appropriate acid‑reducing therapies and avoid potential drug‑interaction pitfalls.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1787
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamin, ambenoniumchlorid and edrofonium are all classified as parasympathomimetic agents, meaning they stimulate the activity of the parasympathetic nervous system. In addition to its parasympathomimetic properties, edrofonium is also recognized for its role as an antidote, indicating that it can be used to counteract the effects of certain toxic exposures or drug overdoses. The taxonomy linking edrofonium to parasympathomimetics suggests that these agents share a common mechanism of action, while edrofonium’s dual classification highlights its therapeutic versatility in both enhancing parasympathetic function and providing antidotal protection. Understanding these relationships aids clinicians in selecting appropriate agents for conditions requiring parasympathetic stimulation and in managing situations where an antidotal effect is needed.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 720
retrieved_docs.content: H2‑receptor antagonists constitute a pharmacologic class that includes the drugs cimetidine, nizatidine and roxatidine. These agents function as histamine H2 antagonists, blocking H2 receptors in the stomach to suppress gastric acid secretion. Cimetidine is chemically related to guanidine, reflecting a structural connection between the drug and the guanidine moiety. The class of H2‑receptor antagonists is itself part of the broader category of histamine H2‑antagonist agents, underscoring the hierarchical relationship among these medications and their shared mechanism of action in the treatment of acid‑related gastrointestinal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1367
retrieved_docs.content: Skopolamin produces a physiological effect of decreasing histamine activity, and this reduction in histamine activity can modulate histamine‑mediated processes such as allergic reactions and gastric acid secretion, contributing to the drug’s overall therapeutic profile.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2794
retrieved_docs.content: Aspirin and ranitidin are frequently noted to co‑occur, indicating that these two agents are often used together or observed in the same clinical contexts. This co‑occurrence suggests that ranitidin may be administered alongside aspirin, potentially to mitigate gastrointestinal side effects associated with aspirin therapy, reflecting an integrated approach to patient care when both medications are indicated.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1387
retrieved_docs.content: Papaverine and its derivatives that are employed in the treatment of functional gastrointestinal disorders are classified as drugs for functional gastrointestinal disorders and are members of the papaverin group.
